structData.title: AHA/ASA 2019 Guidelines for the Early Management of Patients with Acute Ischemic Stroke
structData.source: American Heart Association (AHA) / American Stroke Association (ASA)
structData.description: Comprehensive guidelines for hyperacute stroke evaluation, IV alteplase eligibility, mechanical thrombectomy, blood pressure management, and secondary prevention in acute ischemic stroke.
recommendations: TIME IS BRAIN: Door-to-needle time target <60 minutes; door-to-groin puncture target <90 minutes for thrombectomy
STROKE CODE ACTIVATION: Immediate CT head without contrast to exclude hemorrhage before any thrombolytic therapy
IV ALTEPLASE ELIGIBILITY: Administer within 4.5 hours of symptom onset (or last known well) if no contraindications; 0.9 mg/kg IV, max 90mg, 10% bolus then 60-minute infusion
ALTEPLASE CONTRAINDICATIONS: Active intracranial hemorrhage, major surgery within 14 days, BP >185/110 despite treatment, INR >1.7, platelet count <100,000, blood glucose <50 or >400 mg/dL
BLOOD PRESSURE MANAGEMENT: Lower BP to <185/110 before alteplase; after alteplase maintain <180/105 for 24 hours; for no-thrombolysis patients tolerate up to 220/120 for first 24 hours
MECHANICAL THROMBECTOMY: Indicated for large vessel occlusion (ICA, M1, M2 segments) within 24 hours of onset with favorable imaging profile (ASPECTS score >=6)
THROMBECTOMY IMAGING: CT angiography or MR angiography to confirm large vessel occlusion; CT perfusion or MRI DWI/FLAIR mismatch for extended window (6-24 hours)
ANTIPLATELET THERAPY: Aspirin 325mg within 24-48 hours of onset (delay 24 hours after alteplase); dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) for minor stroke or high-risk TIA for 21 days
ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: Anticoagulation for cardioembolic stroke — start 4-14 days after onset depending on infarct size and hemorrhagic transformation risk
STATIN THERAPY: High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80mg) for all ischemic stroke regardless of baseline LDL
GLYCEMIC CONTROL: Treat hyperglycemia >180 mg/dL; avoid hypoglycemia; target glucose 140-180 mg/dL in acute phase
SWALLOWING SCREEN: Formal dysphagia screen before oral intake — aspiration precautions if impaired
TEMPERATURE: Treat fever (>37.5°C) aggressively — fever worsens penumbra; acetaminophen first-line
STROKE UNIT CARE: Admission to dedicated stroke unit reduces mortality and disability by 20-25% versus general medical ward
structData.keywords: ischemic stroke acute stroke alteplase tPA thrombolysis thrombectomy large vessel occlusion atrial fibrillation aphasia hemiplegia NIHSS last known well door to needle ASPECTS

structData.title: AASLD Position Paper: The Management of Acute Liver Failure — Update 2011
structData.source: American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases (AASLD)
structData.description: Comprehensive management guidelines for acute liver failure (ALF), including etiology-specific therapy, N-acetylcysteine for acetaminophen toxicity, coagulopathy management, hepatic encephalopathy, cerebral edema, and liver transplant criteria.
recommendations: ALF DEFINITION: Severe acute liver injury with coagulopathy (INR >=1.5) and any degree of hepatic encephalopathy in a patient without pre-existing liver disease

ACETAMINOPHEN TOXICITY: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the antidote — 150 mg/kg IV loading dose over 15-60 minutes, then 50 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours; initiate regardless of acetaminophen level if ingestion suspected within 24 hours
NAC FOR NON-ACETAMINOPHEN ALF: IV NAC improves transplant-free survival in early-stage non-acetaminophen ALF (Grade I-II encephalopathy) — recommended by AASLD
ACETAMINOPHEN OVERDOSE THRESHOLD: Rumack-Matthew nomogram — initiate NAC if acetaminophen level above treatment line; treat empirically if time of ingestion unknown
COAGULOPATHY: Do NOT correct INR with FFP unless active bleeding or invasive procedure planned — INR is a prognostic marker; routine FFP transfusion may mask disease progression
HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY: Lactulose NOT shown to benefit ALF (differs from chronic liver disease); rifaximin adjunctive; avoid sedation; continuous EEG if Grade III-IV encephalopathy

CEREBRAL EDEMA: Occurs in up to 80% of Grade IV ALF; osmotherapy with mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV bolus for ICP >25 mmHg; hypertonic saline (NaCl 3-30%) for refractory elevation; target serum sodium 145-155 mEq/L
ICP MONITORING: Consider for Grade III-IV encephalopathy in transplant candidates; risks include hemorrhage into coagulopathic patients
RENAL REPLACEMENT THERAPY: Continuous RRT preferred over intermittent hemodialysis for ALF with renal failure — better hemodynamic tolerance and ICP control
INFECTION: Daily surveillance cultures; low threshold for empiric antibiotics in deteriorating patients — ALF patients are profoundly immunosuppressed; antifungal prophylaxis in severe cases

LIVER TRANSPLANT CRITERIA (King's College): Acetaminophen: arterial pH <7.3 after resuscitation OR all three of INR >6.5, creatinine >3.4 mg/dL, Grade III-IV encephalopathy; Non-acetaminophen: INR >6.5 alone OR any three of age <10 or >40, non-A non-B hepatitis or drug-induced etiology, jaundice >7 days before encephalopathy, INR >3.5, bilirubin >17.5 mg/dL
GLYCEMIC CONTROL: Hypoglycemia common due to hepatic gluconeogenesis failure — check glucose every 1-2 hours; maintain glucose 80-160 mg/dL with dextrose infusion
VASOPRESSORS: Norepinephrine first-line for hemodynamic instability; relative adrenal insufficiency common in ALF — consider hydrocortisone if vasopressor-refractory hypotension
TRANSFER TO TRANSPLANT CENTER: Transfer immediately upon ALF diagnosis — do not wait for clinical deterioration; prognosis changes rapidly
structData.keywords: acute liver failure ALF acetaminophen paracetamol N-acetylcysteine NAC hepatic encephalopathy cerebral edema INR coagulopathy liver transplant King's College criteria fulminant hepatic failure jaundice

structData.title: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in Chronic Kidney Disease + ADA Standards of Care
structData.source: Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) / American Diabetes Association (ADA)
structData.description: Comprehensive guidelines for management of type 2 diabetes in patients with chronic kidney disease, including medication safety thresholds, cardiorenal protection, glycemic targets, and management of AKI superimposed on CKD.
recommendations: METFORMIN CONTRAINDICATION: Metformin is CONTRAINDICATED when eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² due to risk of lactic acidosis — DISCONTINUE IMMEDIATELY
METFORMIN DOSE REDUCTION: Reduce metformin dose by 50% when eGFR 30-45; reassess every 3 months
METFORMIN AND AKI: Hold metformin during any acute illness or procedure that may cause acute kidney injury
PREFERRED AGENT CKD+T2DM: SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) with eGFR >=20 for cardiorenal protection — first-line after metformin discontinuation
GLP-1 RECEPTOR AGONIST: Semaglutide or liraglutide appropriate for CKD stage 3-4 with cardiovascular disease
GLYCEMIC TARGET CKD: HbA1c target 7-8% for CKD stage 4-5 to minimize hypoglycemia risk
SULFONYLUREAS: Use with extreme caution in CKD — glipizide preferred over glyburide; reduce dose by 50% for eGFR <30; HIGH hypoglycemia risk
HYPERKALEMIA MANAGEMENT: Monitor potassium closely; ACE inhibitor/ARB may worsen hyperkalemia in CKD stage 4-5
FLUID MANAGEMENT AKI-on-CKD: Restrict sodium to <2g/day; restrict fluids if oliguric; furosemide for hypervolemia
SULFONAMIDE ALLERGY AND FUROSEMIDE: Furosemide contains sulfonamide moiety — theoretical cross-reactivity with sulfonamide allergy; use bumetanide or ethacrynic acid as alternative if allergy severe
BLOOD PRESSURE TARGET: <130/80 mmHg for CKD with diabetes; ACE inhibitor or ARB first-line for proteinuria
AVOID NEPHROTOXINS: NSAIDs, IV contrast, aminoglycosides — contraindicated or use with extreme caution in CKD stage 4
DIALYSIS PREPARATION: Begin dialysis education for CKD stage 4 (eGFR 15-29); consider AV fistula placement
AKI REVERSAL: Identify and treat reversible causes — obstruction, contrast nephropathy, volume depletion, nephrotoxins
structData.keywords: chronic kidney disease CKD diabetes metformin eGFR AKI acute kidney injury hyperkalemia furosemide SGLT2 sulfonamide allergy lactic acidosis hypervolemia

structData.title: GOLD 2023 Report: Global Strategy for Prevention, Diagnosis and Management of COPD — Acute Exacerbation Chapter
structData.source: Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD)
structData.description: Evidence-based management of acute exacerbations of COPD (AECOPD) including bronchodilator therapy, systemic corticosteroids, antibiotic selection, controlled oxygen therapy, NIV, and discharge criteria.
recommendations: CONTROLLED OXYGEN: Target SpO2 88-92% in COPD exacerbation — avoid over-oxygenation which suppresses hypoxic drive and worsens hypercapnia
SHORT-ACTING BRONCHODILATORS: Salbutamol (albuterol) 2.5mg nebulized every 20 minutes x 3 doses, then every 1-4 hours; add ipratropium 0.5mg nebulized every 6 hours
SYSTEMIC CORTICOSTEROIDS: Prednisolone 40mg orally daily x 5 days (equivalent to IV methylprednisolone); reduces treatment failure and length of stay

ANTIBIOTICS: Indicated when sputum is purulent (increased volume and purulence); amoxicillin-clavulanate or doxycycline for outpatient; azithromycin or levofloxacin for hospitalized; anti-pseudomonal coverage if risk factors
PSEUDOMONAL RISK FACTORS: Frequent exacerbations (>=4/year), prior Pseudomonas isolation, FEV1 <30% predicted, recent hospitalization — use piperacillin-tazobactam or ciprofloxacin
NON-INVASIVE VENTILATION (NIV): BiPAP indicated for pH <7.35 and PaCO2 >45 mmHg; reduces need for intubation and in-hospital mortality
INTUBATION CRITERIA: NIV failure, severe acidosis (pH <7.25), hemodynamic instability, decreased consciousness, inability to protect airway
METHYLXANTHINES: Theophylline NOT recommended in acute exacerbation — no added benefit and significant toxicity risk
SPUTUM CULTURE: Obtain before antibiotics if hospitalized, especially if purulent or ICU admission anticipated
CHEST RADIOGRAPH: Obtain to exclude pneumonia, pneumothorax, and pleural effusion as alternative or complicating diagnoses
ABG MONITORING: Arterial blood gas at presentation and 1-2 hours after initiating oxygen or NIV to assess response and guide ventilatory support
VENOUS THROMBOEMBOLISM PROPHYLAXIS: LMWH or unfractionated heparin for all hospitalized COPD exacerbations — high VTE risk during acute illness
DISCHARGE CRITERIA: SpO2 stable on baseline oxygen, able to use inhaler independently, no need for parenteral bronchodilators >4 hours, walking ability, adequate home support
PULMONARY REHABILITATION: Begin within 4 weeks of discharge — reduces re-hospitalization and mortality
structData.keywords: COPD chronic obstructive pulmonary disease acute exacerbation AECOPD bronchodilator corticosteroids NIV BiPAP hypercapnia purulent sputum pneumonia controlled oxygen albuterol ipratropium

structData.title: ADA Consensus Statement: Diabetic Ketoacidosis and Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State in Adults
structData.description: Evidence-based management of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) in adults including fluid resuscitation, insulin therapy, electrolyte replacement, bicarbonate use, and transition to subcutaneous insulin.
recommendations: DKA DIAGNOSIS CRITERIA: Glucose >250 mg/dL, arterial pH <7.3, serum bicarbonate <18 mEq/L, positive urine or serum ketones, anion gap >12
FLUID RESUSCITATION: 1-1.5L normal saline (0.9% NaCl) IV in first hour; subsequent rate 250-500 mL/hr; switch to 0.45% NaCl once glucose <250 mg/dL
INSULIN THERAPY: Do NOT start insulin until potassium >=3.5 mEq/L; begin 0.1 units/kg/hr regular insulin infusion; reduce to 0.05 units/kg/hr when glucose reaches 200 mg/dL
POTASSIUM REPLACEMENT: Add 20-40 mEq/L KCl to IV fluids if potassium 3.5-5.0 mEq/L; replace aggressively if K+ <3.5 — hold insulin and replace first
GLUCOSE IN IV FLUIDS: Add dextrose (5% or 10%) to IV fluids when glucose <200 mg/dL to allow continued insulin infusion and ketone clearance
BICARBONATE: NOT routinely recommended; consider only if pH <6.9 — may worsen hypokalemia and impair cerebral oxygen delivery
PHOSPHATE REPLACEMENT: Replace if phosphate <1 mg/dL or if cardiac dysfunction, hemolytic anemia, or respiratory depression present
ANION GAP MONITORING: Check glucose, electrolytes, and anion gap every 2-4 hours; DKA resolution requires anion gap <12 and bicarbonate >=18
TRANSITION TO SUBCUTANEOUS INSULIN: When patient tolerating oral intake, anion gap closed, and bicarbonate >=18 — overlap subcutaneous basal insulin by 2 hours before stopping infusion
PRECIPITATING CAUSE: Identify and treat trigger — infection (most common), insulin non-compliance, new-onset T1DM, medications (SGLT2 inhibitors), myocardial infarction
CEREBRAL EDEMA: Most common in pediatric DKA; risk factors include bicarbonate use and rapid fluid correction; treat with mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV
EUGLYCEMIC DKA: SGLT2 inhibitor-induced DKA — glucose may be normal or mildly elevated; check ketones in any patient on SGLT2 inhibitor with nausea or vomiting
CARDIAC MONITORING: Continuous telemetry during insulin and potassium infusion — hypokalemia and hyperkalemia both cause life-threatening dysrhythmias
ICU CRITERIA: pH <7.0, altered mental status, hemodynamic instability, or K+ <3.0 warrant ICU-level monitoring
structData.keywords: diabetic ketoacidosis DKA hyperglycemia ketones anion gap insulin infusion potassium replacement Kussmaul breathing euglycemic DKA SGLT2 fluid resuscitation type 1 diabetes type 2 diabetes

structData.title: AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Febrile Seizures — Neurodiagnostic Evaluation and Long-Term Management
structData.description: Evidence-based guidelines for the evaluation and management of simple and complex febrile seizures in children aged 6 months to 5 years, including LP indications, neuroimaging, EEG, and recurrence counseling.
recommendations: SIMPLE FEBRILE SEIZURE DEFINITION: Single seizure lasting <15 minutes, generalized (not focal), occurring in child 6 months to 5 years with fever >=38°C, no CNS infection, no prior afebrile seizure, returns to baseline within 1 hour
COMPLEX FEBRILE SEIZURE: Focal features, duration >15 minutes, or multiple seizures within 24 hours — requires more extensive evaluation

LUMBAR PUNCTURE: NOT routinely indicated for simple febrile seizure in fully immunized child; consider LP if meningeal signs present, prolonged post-ictal state, complex febrile seizure, age <12 months (especially if pre-vaccination era), or pre-treated with antibiotics
NEUROIMAGING: CT or MRI NOT routinely indicated for simple febrile seizure; obtain if focal neurological deficit, increased ICP signs, complex febrile seizure, or failure to return to baseline
EEG: NOT recommended for simple febrile seizure — does not predict recurrence or future epilepsy risk
BLOOD TESTS: Serum glucose, electrolytes, CBC NOT routinely indicated unless clinical concern for metabolic cause or dehydration; obtain if ill-appearing child
FEVER MANAGEMENT: Antipyretics (acetaminophen or ibuprofen) for comfort — do NOT prevent febrile seizure recurrence; do not use prophylactically
ACTIVE SEIZURE MANAGEMENT: Benzodiazepine (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV or diazepam 0.5 mg/kg PR) if seizure lasting >5 minutes or second seizure occurs
RECURRENCE RISK: 30-35% overall recurrence risk; highest if age <18 months, temperature <40°C at first seizure, short duration of fever before seizure, family history of febrile seizures
EPILEPSY RISK: Simple febrile seizure carries <2% lifetime risk of developing epilepsy — only slightly above general population baseline (1%); complex febrile seizure risk 4-6%
PROPHYLACTIC ANTICONVULSANTS: NOT recommended for simple febrile seizures — risks outweigh benefits; consider intermittent diazepam for high-recurrence-risk families if significant parental anxiety
DISCHARGE CRITERIA: Seizure terminated, child returned to neurological baseline, fever source identified, no signs of meningitis or encephalitis, adequate parental education
PARENTAL COUNSELING: Reassure that simple febrile seizures do not cause brain damage; educate on seizure first aid — position child safely, time seizure, call EMS if >5 minutes
structData.keywords: febrile seizure pediatric seizure fever convulsion meningitis lumbar puncture benzodiazepine lorazepam diazepam recurrence epilepsy child pediatric AAP

structData.title: ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Patients With Non-ST-Elevation Acute Coronary Syndromes (NSTE-ACS)
structData.source: American College of Cardiology / American Heart Association
structData.description: Evidence-based guidelines for risk stratification, antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, invasive strategy, and secondary prevention in patients with NSTEMI and unstable angina. Includes specific guidance for allergy management in ACS.
recommendations: DUAL ANTIPLATELET THERAPY (DAPT): Aspirin 325mg loading dose PLUS P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor 180mg or clopidogrel 600mg) for all NSTE-ACS patients
ASPIRIN ALLERGY PROTOCOL: If true aspirin allergy — do NOT administer aspirin; use P2Y12 inhibitor monotherapy (ticagrelor preferred) plus consider aspirin desensitization
ASPIRIN ALLERGY — ALTERNATIVE: Ticagrelor 180mg loading dose as monotherapy is acceptable when aspirin is contraindicated due to allergy
ASPIRIN HYPERSENSITIVITY: Aspirin desensitization protocol may be performed in catheterization lab setting under monitoring if aspirin essential for stenting
Obtain 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of ED arrival; repeat every 15-30 min if initial non-diagnostic
Measure high-sensitivity troponin at presentation and 1-3 hours later (0/1hr or 0/3hr algorithm)
Risk stratification using TIMI or GRACE score within 2 hours of presentation
Anticoagulation: UFH or enoxaparin for all NSTE-ACS; fondaparinux preferred if no PCI planned
Early invasive strategy (angiography within 24h) for high-risk features: elevated troponin, dynamic ECG changes, GRACE score >140
Statin therapy: high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80mg) immediately upon diagnosis
Beta-blocker within 24 hours if no contraindications (EF >=40%, no acute HF, no bradycardia)
ACE inhibitor/ARB for all NSTE-ACS with EF <40%, hypertension, or diabetes
Avoid NSAIDs and COX-2 inhibitors in ACS — increased cardiovascular risk
Secondary prevention: smoking cessation, cardiac rehabilitation, lipid management to LDL <70 mg/dL
structData.keywords: NSTEMI NSTE-ACS unstable angina troponin aspirin allergy dual antiplatelet ticagrelor clopidogrel PCI coronary angiography acute coronary syndrome

structData.title: ACOG Practice Bulletin 183: Postpartum Hemorrhage
structData.source: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG)
structData.evidence_level: Level A — Good and Consistent Evidence
structData.description: Evidence-based management of postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) including uterotonic therapy, surgical interventions, transfusion protocols, and management of consumptive coagulopathy.
recommendations: PPH DEFINITION: Blood loss >=1000 mL within 24 hours of delivery OR blood loss associated with signs or symptoms of hypovolemia
UTERINE ATONY (most common cause, 80% of PPH): Bimanual uterine massage plus oxytocin 10-40 units in 500-1000 mL NS at 100-150 mL/hr IV

SECOND-LINE UTEROTONICS: Methylergonovine 0.2mg IM (contraindicated in hypertension); carboprost (15-methyl PGF2-alpha) 250mcg IM every 15-90 min, max 8 doses (contraindicated in asthma); misoprostol 800-1000mcg sublingual or rectal
TRANEXAMIC ACID: 1g IV over 10-20 minutes within 3 hours of hemorrhage onset — reduces PPH mortality by 30%; repeat 1g if bleeding continues at 30 minutes
MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL: Activate at EBL >1500 mL or ongoing hemorrhage; target 1:1:1 ratio of packed red blood cells to fresh frozen plasma to platelets
FIBRINOGEN REPLACEMENT: Give cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate if fibrinogen <200 mg/dL — fibrinogen is consumed early in obstetric hemorrhage
BALLOON TAMPONADE: Intrauterine balloon (Bakri balloon) with 300-500 mL saline fill for refractory atony after uterotonics — test for ongoing bleeding before surgical escalation
UTERINE COMPRESSION SUTURES: B-Lynch or Hayman suture for refractory atony when uterus responds to bimanual compression
UTERINE ARTERY EMBOLIZATION: Interventional radiology option for hemodynamically stable patients with refractory hemorrhage when fertility preservation desired
HYSTERECTOMY: Definitive treatment for refractory PPH when all conservative measures fail — do not delay to avoid mortality
COAGULOPATHY MANAGEMENT: Point-of-care testing (TEG or ROTEM) guides product replacement in real-time; treat DIC aggressively with FFP, cryoprecipitate, and platelets
CALCIUM: Administer calcium gluconate 1g IV with every 4 units of packed red blood cells to prevent citrate-induced hypocalcemia during massive transfusion
TEMPERATURE AND ACIDOSIS: Maintain temperature >36°C and pH >7.2 — hypothermia and acidosis worsen coagulopathy (lethal triad with hypovolemia)
CAUSE IDENTIFICATION: 4 Ts — Tone (atony), Trauma (lacerations, hematoma), Tissue (retained placenta), Thrombin (coagulopathy)
structData.keywords: postpartum hemorrhage PPH uterine atony oxytocin methylergonovine carboprost misoprostol tranexamic acid massive transfusion DIC Bakri balloon hysterectomy obstetric hemorrhage EBL

structData.title: ESC 2019 Guidelines on Acute Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis and Management
structData.source: European Society of Cardiology (ESC) / European Respiratory Society (ERS)
structData.description: Evidence-based guidelines for risk stratification, anticoagulation, reperfusion therapy, and long-term management of acute pulmonary embolism, including massive, submassive, and low-risk presentations.
recommendations: IMMEDIATE ANTICOAGULATION: Start anticoagulation without delay in high or intermediate clinical probability — do not wait for imaging confirmation
DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING: CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) is the gold standard for PE confirmation in hemodynamically stable patients
WELLS SCORE: Use Wells criteria to stratify pre-test probability; score >4 indicates high probability, proceed directly to CTPA
D-DIMER: PERC rule if low clinical probability; if PERC negative, no further workup needed; if positive, obtain age-adjusted D-dimer

MASSIVE PE (hemodynamically unstable): Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100mg IV over 2 hours is first-line; consider surgical embolectomy or catheter-directed thrombolysis if thrombolysis contraindicated
SUBMASSIVE PE (RV dysfunction, hemodynamically stable): Anticoagulation alone; escalate to reperfusion if clinical deterioration
LOW-RISK PE: DOAC monotherapy preferred — rivaroxaban 15mg twice daily x 21 days then 20mg daily, or apixaban 10mg twice daily x 7 days then 5mg twice daily
ANTICOAGULATION DURATION: Minimum 3 months for provoked PE; extended therapy (indefinite) for unprovoked PE or malignancy-associated PE
CANCER-ASSOCIATED PE: LMWH (dalteparin) or rivaroxaban preferred over warfarin for malignancy-associated VTE
INFERIOR VENA CAVA FILTER: Only when anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated; retrieve when anticoagulation becomes feasible
ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY: Bedside TTE for hemodynamically unstable patients to assess RV function and exclude alternative diagnoses
TROPONIN AND BNP: Elevated troponin or BNP indicates RV strain — intermediate-high risk category; consider ICU monitoring
POST-PE FOLLOW-UP: Repeat echocardiography at 3-6 months; evaluate for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) if persistent dyspnea
DVT WORKUP: Bilateral lower extremity compression ultrasound if CTPA non-diagnostic or radiation concern
structData.keywords: pulmonary embolism PE DVT deep vein thrombosis anticoagulation rivaroxaban apixaban thrombolysis alteplase Wells score D-dimer CTPA right ventricular dysfunction VTE

structData.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2021
structData.source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign / Society of Critical Care Medicine
structData.description: Comprehensive evidence-based guidelines for the recognition, resuscitation, and management of adult patients with sepsis and septic shock, including antibiotic stewardship, fluid resuscitation, vasopressor use, and source control.
recommendations: HOUR-1 BUNDLE: Begin resuscitation immediately upon recognition of sepsis or septic shock
Measure lactate level; re-measure lactate if initial lactate >2 mmol/L
Obtain blood cultures BEFORE administering antibiotics
Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of sepsis recognition
Begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid fluid for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L
Apply vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) for MAP <65 mmHg despite fluids
For septic shock: target MAP >=65 mmHg with norepinephrine as first-line vasopressor
For urinary source (pyelonephritis/urosepsis): ceftriaxone 1-2g IV q24h is first-line UNLESS penicillin allergy — use aztreonam or fluoroquinolone instead
PENICILLIN ALLERGY NOTE: Ceftriaxone has 1-2% cross-reactivity with penicillin allergy — assess allergy history before administration
Reassess fluid responsiveness with dynamic measures; avoid fluid overload
Source control: drain abscess, remove infected device, or treat obstruction within 6-12 hours
Procalcitonin-guided antibiotic de-escalation recommended to reduce duration
Target 7-day antibiotic course for uncomplicated bacteremia from urinary source
structData.keywords: sepsis septic shock pyelonephritis urosepsis bacteremia hypotension lactate norepinephrine broad spectrum antibiotics
